hook.text: A five-year veteran discovers the new hire makes more than they do. Same role. Less experience. It's called compression.

buildup.text: Pay compression happens when new hires are offered market-rate salaries that match or exceed long-tenured employees' pay.

discovery.text: It occurs when market rates rise faster than internal raises—creating shrinking gaps between junior and senior pay.

twist.text: Companies that ignore compression lose their best tenured people—who find out, feel undervalued, and leave for market rate.

climax.text: Regular equity adjustments—reviewing and correcting internal pay gaps—prevent compression from becoming a retention crisis.

punchline.text: If you pay the market to hire, pay the market to keep.

quiz.question: What causes pay compression?
options: New hires getting market rates that match or exceed veterans' pay
